g.text: TIME-SERIES WORKLOADS: have very specific access patterns that general-purpose DBs do poorly

text.tspan: WRITE:
u2022 append-only, sequential, very high QPS (100ku20131M+/sec)
u2022 always 'now' u2014 keys are monotonically increasing timestamps
u2022 essentially no updates or deletes (data immutable after write)
READ:
u2022 by time range ('last 5 minutes', 'last 30 days at 1-hour resolution')
u2022 grouped by tag/dimension ('CPU per host', 'latency per endpoint')
u2022 aggregated (sum, avg, p99) over the range
u2022 rarely point lookups; almost always range scans + aggregation
RETENTION:
u2022 raw data for hours/days; downsampled for months/years
u2022 delete oldest as part of normal operation
u2022 Time-partitioned storage u2014 each chunk holds one time window
u2192 cheap deletion of expired chunks (drop the partition)
u2192 efficient range queries (scan only relevant partitions)
u2022 Heavy compression u2014 timestamps and metric values are highly compressible
(delta-of-delta encoding, Gorilla compression u2014 100u00d7 better than naive)
u2022 Downsampling rollups u2014 maintain pre-aggregated lower-resolution copies
u2192 5s-resolution kept for 1 day; 1m for 30 days; 1h for 1 year
u2022 Retention policies u2014 auto-drop chunks older than N days
u2022 Cardinality awareness u2014 every tag combination creates a unique series;
TSDBs index by series ID, not row
u2022 Specialized query languages (PromQL, InfluxQL, Flux, M3QL) tailored for
time-aware operations: rate(), increase(), windowing, alignment
u2022 Prometheus u2014 monitoring; pull-based; PromQL
u2022 InfluxDB u2014 general TSDB; push or pull
u2022 TimescaleDB u2014 Postgres extension; SQL-friendly
u2022 VictoriaMetrics, M3, Cortex, Thanos u2014 Prometheus at scale
u2022 QuestDB u2014 SQL-first TSDB; very fast
u2022 KDB+ u2014 financial market data; proprietary; expensive
u2022 Druid, Pinot u2014 OLAP-style time-aware
u2022 ClickHouse u2014 column store popular for time-series and logs
